turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: In case 17-1471, Home Depot v. Jackson, Justice Thomas has the opinion of the Court.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on a writ of certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Fourth Circuit. Citibank filed a debt collection action in State Court, alleging that respondent George Jackson was liable for charges incurred on a Home Depot credit card. As relevant here, Jackson responded by filing class action claims against a third-party, petitioner Home Depot. Home Depot filed a notice to remove the case from state to Federal Court, but Jackson moved to remand, arguing that a third-party counterclaim defendant like Home Depot could not remove. The District Court granted Jackson's Motion to Remand. The Fourth Circuit affirmed. It held that neither the general removal provision, 28 U.S.C. §1441(a), nor the removal provision in the Class Action Fairness Act, 28 U.S.C. §1453(b) allowed Home Depot to remove the class action claims filed against them. In an opinion filed with the clerk today, we affirm. Because the term defendant as used in §1441(a) and §1453(b) refers only to the party sued by the original plaintiffs, the provisions do not allow third-party counterclaim defendant like Home Depot to remove. §1441(a) provides that any civil action over which a Federal Court could or would have original jurisdiction may be removed to Federal Court by the defendant or the defendants. This Court has long held that a District Court when determining whether it has original jurisdiction over a civil action must evaluate whether the plaintiff could have filed the operative complaint in Federal Court. The civil action over which the District Court must have original jurisdiction is thus the action as defined by the plaintiff's complaint. The defendant described in the statutes, therefore the defendant to that complaint, not a party named in a counterclaim. This conclusion is bolstered by the use of the term defendant and related context. For example, the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure differentiate between third-party defendants, counterclaim defendants, and defendants. And in other removal provisions Congress has clearly extended removal authority to parties other than the original defendant. It has not done so here. This determination is also consistent with our holding in Shamrock Oil & Gas Corp. v. Sheets. If, as we held there, a counterclaim defendant, who was the original plaintiff, is not one of the defendants, then there is no textual reason to reach a different conclusion for a counterclaim defendant who was not a part of the initial lawsuit. Home Depot contends that even if §1441(a) does not permit removal here, §1453(b) does, because it uses the phrase any defendant instead of the defendant. We disagree. §1453(b) provides that a class action may be removed by any defendant without the consent of all defendants and without regard to whether any defendant is a citizen of the state in which the action is brought. These two clauses simply clarify that certain limitations on removal that would otherwise apply do not limit removal under §1453(b). Neither of these clauses, nor anything else in the statute alters §1441(a)'s limitations on who can remove, suggesting that Congress intended to leave that limit in place. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we affirm the judgment of the Fourth Circuit. Justice Alito has filed a dissenting opinion, which is joined by the Chief Justice and Justices Gorsuch and Kavanaugh.
